solution: The divergence is \( \partial_x P + \partial_y Q + \partial_z R \). Only the \( x \)-term of \( P \), the \( y \)-term of \( Q \), and the \( z \)-term of \( R \) survive (the cross terms differentiate to 0), so \( \nabla\cdot\mathbf{F} = ([[a]]) + ([[b]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This sums every coefficient in the field; the cross terms \( ([[d]])y,\ ([[e]])z,\ ([[f]])x \) vanish under the divergence.
This keeps only the cross-term coefficients, which contribute nothing to the divergence.
This multiplies the three diagonal coefficients instead of adding them.
This is off by one from the correct divergence.
This is off by one from the correct divergence.
This is off by two from the correct divergence.
This is off by two from the correct divergence.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( ([[a]])x^{2},\; ([[b]])y^{2},\; ([[c]])z^{2} \right) \). Compute the divergence \( \nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 that comes from differentiating each squared term.
This evaluates the divergence formula at the coordinates cyclically shifted, mismatching each partial with the wrong coordinate.
This has the sign of the divergence reversed.
This is the divergence of the LINEAR analogue field, not this quadratic one; it forgets the point evaluation entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( kx,\; ([[a]])y + ([[p]])z,\; ([[b]])z \right) \), where \( k \) is a constant. For what value of \( k \) is \( \mathbf{F} \) divergence-free (\( \nabla\cdot\mathbf{F}=0 \) everywhere)?
solution: The divergence is \( \nabla\cdot\mathbf{F} = k + ([[a]]) + ([[b]]) \) (the cross term \( ([[p]])z \) in the middle component does not depend on \( y \), so it drops out). Setting this to 0 and solving gives \( k = -\left(([[a]]) + ([[b]])\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign, solving \( k = a+b \) instead of \( k=-(a+b) \).
This subtracts instead of adds the two known coefficients before negating.
This negates only the first coefficient.
This is the unrelated cross-term coefficient \( p \), which never affects the divergence.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
This is off by two from the correct value of \( k \).
